summary: Fernando Pérez delivers a keynote on the sustainable development of Jupyter and open scientific tools, tracing the project's evolution from a 260-line Python script to a multi-language platform used by millions. He demonstrates JupyterLab's modular architecture, JupyterHub's deployment capabilities, and Binder's one-click reproducibility. He shares how he taught reproducible data science practices to undergraduates at UC Berkeley, turning them into everyday habits rather than afterthoughts. The talk concludes with a frank examination of the tensions between open source sustainability, academic incentives, and industry's extractive relationship with open source communities.
key_points: Jupyter's formalized protocol approach paid off by enabling ~100 programming language backends and a composable architecture in JupyterLab that third parties can build upon
Binder transforms reproducible research from aspiration to one-click reality by packaging code, dependencies, and data into executable environments from a single URL
Teaching reproducible practices as everyday habits (like brushing teeth) to undergraduates proved transformative, with students contributing to open source and pursuing data science careers
Industry has a largely extractive relationship with open source — mining free technology and talent trained at no cost to them, which amplifies diversity problems and threatens project sustainability
Open source communities must evolve from a rebel mindset to one of deliberate, systematic community building now that they have real influence and responsibility
money_quotes: Regardless of how much the internet advances, regardless of how many data centers Google or Amazon open, we are still bounded by the bag of meat in our heads.

What we try to do with IPython and Jupyter is basically build a bridge between those two worlds, build tools that help you have a dialogue with a computer to reason about problems and to have dialogues with each other.
Making your paper reproducible at publication time is the root canal of science, because by that point you are probably in for a huge amount of pain if you need to go back and clean up years of work.

There's a lot of industry that has a purely extractive mindset within its relationship with the open source world. It mines extractively the technology itself. It mines the people who get trained for free. It's not free, by the way. It's just that they're not paying for it.
That practice is extremely toxic to building a more inclusive community because who can afford to work for free?
Right now, Google, Amazon, Microsoft, IBM, all have commercial products based on Jupyter, and we're not exactly getting rich around here. We're still scrambling for small grants.
If you felt that you were the young rebel once, and your act of rebellion because it was morally justified could justify all other things, well, for us, it's kind of time to outgrow that mindset.
key_insight: Open source scientific tools have reached maturity and widespread adoption, but their sustainability demands that both communities and industry move beyond extractive patterns toward deliberate, equitable models of support and governance.
